verse_text: then you will begin to say, 'We ate and drank in Your presence, and You taught in our streets.'

links.context: The description of the two ways was widespread in Judaism ([[DEU.30:15]]-20; Ps 1; Pr 4:17, 18; 12:28; 15:23; WSir 15:17) and in early Christian writings (Didache, Barnabas). Luke's version ([[LUK.13:24]]-30) is more eschatological, referring to the end of the age. Because we wrestle against sins and human weaknesses as well as spiritual forces of evil ([[EPH.6:12]]), entering the Kingdom is the more difficult way.

I will... dine with him if there is cooperation, the synergy of an open door. God does not force anyone to cooperate with Him. He keeps knocking, awaiting a response. This promise looks toward both the messianic banquet, the wedding supper of the Lamb of God (19:9; [[LUK.22:30]]), and the Holy Eucharist, which is a foretaste of the heavenly feast. Faithful disciples are promised they will eat and drink at table with Him ([[LUK.22:28]]-30) in the ultimate reality of the Kingdom of heaven. Note the parallels with the parable of the bridegroom ([[MAT.25:1]]-13) and with the teaching about those who will shut themselves out of the feast of the Kingdom ([[LUK.13:25]]-30).
